title: Draft -> Post workflow
body: Buffer Sign in with Buffer Feature Suggestions Roadmap What's New 15 15 Draft -> Post workflow It is confusing that a draft stays in drafts, even after you have created a post based on that draft. A more intuitive flow would be that the draft moves out of the ideas tab, and only lives as a post. Now I have to go back to drafts and delete once I have finished scheduling the post - which the first few times gives you bit of an anxious feeling - until you have verified that your post is indeed still there after the draft has been deleted. I get that there can be scenarios when you want to retain drafts, if you use them as some sort of templates, but then having the option to choose when creating the post would be great. title: A Functional Buffer Report to PDF Option

title: Select multiple posts at once to drag and drop in another time slot.
body: Buffer Sign in with Buffer Feature Suggestions Roadmap What's New 13 13 Select multiple posts at once to drag and drop in another time slot. Sometimes I want to move multiple posts from one time to another, and when you have 6-8 posts it can be tedious to move each individually. I suggest that you are able to drag your mouse over the intended posts and move all in one go, similar to how you can on windows desktop or in the files app. All the best, Will. Comments Join the discussion and help shape the future of Buffer. Once you’re signed in, you’ll be able to: Vote on posts Share your thoughts React to comments Submit new posts Join the discussion A Admin · about 1 year ago Hey @will - thanks for your suggestion! My team at Buffer is actively exploring some improvements to the queue functionality, including making it easier to use at scale. I wanted to dig a bit more into your suggestion, because I want to ensure I understand how you’d like this bulk-drag-and-drop to work 😄 If we were to make it possible to select multiple posts in your queue, and drag-and-drop them together to a new location, how would you expect those posts to be rescheduled? I could see it working this way: You select 10 posts and we remember the order that they were in You drag them to a new point in your Queue (eg. reschedule for 2 weeks later) The order of the 10 posts is preserved, and we simply line them up chronologically in the available slots starting at that point you dragged the posts to. This could be all in a single day, or across multiple days, depending on how many posting times you’ve set up Is this similar to what you have in mind? I could also see Buffer automatically spacing them out in certain intervals, which could be differently beneficial. Would you expect anything else to happen here? Let me know! title: Extended Team Permissions (Owner / Super Admin / Admin / Member)
body: Buffer Sign in with Buffer Feature Suggestions Roadmap What's New 12 12 Extended Team Permissions (Owner / Super Admin / Admin / Member) I’d like to give trusted team members (like our Content Director) expanded permissions — including the ability to connect new social media channels, manage team access, and view/edit all existing channels — without needing to go through the account owner. Right now, only the owner can connect channels or assign access, which creates bottlenecks and limits delegation. To support agency and team workflows better, it would be incredibly helpful to have a more detailed permission structure. For example: Suggested Permissions Hierarchy: Owner Full access to all features and billing Add/remove channels Manage all team members and permissions View and manage all connected social profiles Exclusive access to sensitive account/billing settings Super Admin (e.g., Content Director) Add and connect new social channels View and manage all existing channels Invite and assign team members (but not manage billing) Publish, schedule, edit content across all channels Admin (e.g., Third-party partner like MixBloom) View assigned channels Publish, schedule, and edit content only for assigned channels Cannot connect new channels or manage team permissions Member / Contributor View and draft content for assigned channels Cannot publish, edit others’ content, or manage any settings This would give owners the flexibility to grant higher-level control to trusted internal team members, while maintaining limited access for outside vendors or contributors. Comments Join the discussion and help shape the future of Buffer. title: Group related posts for different channels
body: Buffer Sign in with Buffer Feature Suggestions Roadmap What's New 11 11 Group related posts for different channels When you create a post with the goal of sharing to multiple social networks, each channel-specific instance of the post gets separated out on your calendar. This makes it difficult to track which versions of the post performed best, make edits across each instance before they publish, and it makes the calendar visually hard to parse. It would be great if we could group or cluster posts that are all essentially the same content, to address those pain points. This could allow us to also surface aggregated data and insights around how a post performed across your entire audience, rather than each channel separately. title: Instagram – Support Scheduling 3:4 Media (1080x1440px)
body: Buffer Sign in with Buffer Feature Suggestions Roadmap What's New 10 10 Instagram – Support Scheduling 3:4 Media (1080x1440px) Instagram recently allowed posting pictures and videos in the 3:4 aspect ratio (1080×1440px). It would be great to also be able to schedule media in this format via Buffer. Comments Join the discussion and help shape the future of Buffer. Once you’re signed in, you’ll be able to: Vote on posts Share your thoughts React to comments Submit new posts Join the discussion A Admin · Merged comment · 8 months ago Hi @dina - thanks for reaching out! Unfortunately while this is possible natively, it is not yet supported via Instagram’s API, which means third party applications cannot yet offer it to our users. We will certainly adjust this as soon as we can :) A Admin · 10 months ago Hi @redaktion - thanks for your suggestion! We actually do support this already - are you encountering difficulties uploading this aspect ratio? Please let us know! S Someone · 10 months ago Hi Admin, thanks for your reply. We are, in fact, encountering difficulties with this aspect ratio – specifically, not with uploading but with scheduling for ‘Automatic’ posting on Instagram. The error message in the ‘Create Post’ composer reads as follows: “One or more images aren't matching the expected aspect ratios (4:5 to 1.91:1). Switch your posting from Automatic to Notify Me.” The ‘Schedule Post’ button is also greyed out so that scheduling is simply not possible. A Admin · 9 months ago Hey @redaktion - we’re working on a few changes that will prevent this from happening in the future. In the meantime, would you be able to try this out and see if you’re still encountering issues? We have an editing functionality in our composer and we have 4:5 as one of the presets. I suspect it may be a few pixels off and is therefore not being registered properly, but we will fix this shortly! S Someone · 9 months ago Hi Admin, we’re talking about 3:4 or 1080×1440px – not 4:5 or 1080×1350px. When trying to schedule 3×4 creatives to Instagram, we still get the error message “One or more images aren't matching the expected aspect ratios (4:5 to 1.91:1). Switch your posting from Automatic to Notify Me.” I can also assure you that our creatives use the correct dimensions and are not “a few pixels off.” A Admin · 9 months ago Hi @redaktion - my apologies for misreading your initial request. I’ve looked into their API documentation and it seems like they have not yet supported this aspect ratio for third party clients. We will adjust this as soon as it becomes available to us. If Buffer doesn’t enact it, I have to cancel my membership. A Admin · 18 days ago Hi @spirok75 - thanks for your comment! I recently became aware of this law as well, though from what I understand the law strictly focuses on commercial advertising and social media is a bit of a gray area. That said - we’d like to support a feature to tag posts as AI-generated. title: Engagement Notification in App
body: Buffer Sign in with Buffer Feature Suggestions Roadmap What's New 8 8 Engagement Notification in App This month I decided to make the switch from Sprout to Buffer. One of the features I used in Sprout was post response notifications. title: API for Post Analytics
body: Buffer Sign in with Buffer Feature Suggestions Roadmap What's New 7 7 API for Post Analytics Hi, We are a small company that would like to use your API in order to fetch data from out social media accounts and have a realtime report of how our posts are doing. We already have all the frontend ready, missing the API for this in order to populate it. Is this something that you may offer by any change? Thanks, Flavius Comments Join the discussion and help shape the future of Buffer. Once you’re signed in, you’ll be able to: Vote on posts Share your thoughts React to comments Submit new posts Join the discussion A Admin · 4 months ago Hey @flavius - thanks for your suggestion! We are currently rebuilding our Public API, but we will be limited on what we can share in terms of post analytics. This is due to the terms of service that we agree to with our third-party partners when registering to become a developer (i.e., Meta, X, LinkedIn), and unfortunately, data privacy and exposing this beyond our own app is one thing that is quite difficult to negotiate on. We do have a few networks that are fine with us offering this, but these are the smaller ones. I just wanted to follow up on this to let you know. We will likely support our own Buffer analytics shared via the API at a later stage, which may still be useful to you and your team. S Someone · over 1 year ago I would really like an API for post analytics as well! A Admin · about 2 years ago Hi @Someone - thanks for your suggestion! At this time we do not have a public API; we are exploring the idea of opening one up in the near future, so it’s helpful to hear your specific use case! I’ve added this to our suggestion board for now and will keep it in mind as we define the long-term plans for our API. title: Integration of Personal ChatGPT Plus Accounts into Buffer's AI Assistant
body: Buffer Sign in with Buffer Feature Suggestions Roadmap What's New 7 7 Integration of Personal ChatGPT Plus Accounts into Buffer's AI Assistant Proposal for Integration of Personal ChatGPT Plus Accounts into Buffer's AI Assistant Introduction Buffer has consistently been at the forefront of social media management and analytics, providing users with intuitive tools for content scheduling, analytics, and engagement. With the rapid advancement in AI technology, particularly in natural language processing, there's an opportunity to significantly enhance Buffer's capabilities by integrating OpenAI's ChatGPT Plus model. This proposal outlines the advantages and implementation strategy for allowing users to connect their personal ChatGPT Plus accounts to Buffer's AI assistant. Objective To enhance Buffer's AI assistant by leveraging the advanced capabilities of OpenAI's ChatGPT Plus, enabling users to generate more sophisticated, context-aware, and personalized content for their social media channels. Benefits Advanced Content Generation: ChatGPT Plus offers superior language understanding and generation, allowing for more nuanced and high-quality content creation. Personalization and Brand Voice Consistency: Users can train their ChatGPT Plus model on specific styles or tones, ensuring consistency with their brand's voice across all content. Efficient Workflow: Integrating ChatGPT Plus into Buffer's interface streamlines the content creation process, making it faster and more efficient. Enhanced User Engagement: The improved quality and relevance of content generated through ChatGPT Plus can lead to higher engagement rates on social media platforms. Competitive Edge: This feature would position Buffer as a pioneer in integrating cutting-edge AI technology for social media management. Implementation Strategy User Account Linking: Develop a secure authentication system for users to link their ChatGPT Plus account with their Buffer account. AI Assistant Enhancement: Integrate ChatGPT Plus's API into Buffer's existing AI assistant, ensuring seamless functionality within the current user interface. Customization Options: Allow users to set preferences for how the AI assistant utilizes their ChatGPT Plus capabilities, such as tone, style, and content type preferences. User Education and Support: Provide resources and support to educate users on maximizing the benefits of this integration. Feedback and Iteration: Establish a feedback loop with early adopters to refine and improve the integration based on real-world usage. Conclusion The integration of personal ChatGPT Plus accounts into Buffer's AI assistant represents a significant leap forward in social media management technology. It promises to deliver enhanced content quality, increased efficiency, and a more personalized user experience. We believe this integration aligns perfectly with Buffer's commitment to innovation and customer satisfaction and will set a new standard in the industry. Comments Join the discussion and help shape the future of Buffer. title: Intermediate Editor Permission Level
body: Buffer Sign in with Buffer Feature Suggestions Roadmap What's New 7 7 Intermediate Editor Permission Level I am using Buffer for a school team. The goal is for students to be able to collaborate on a post and then it can be approved for publishing. Currently, Buffer’s ‘Approval Required’ role does not really support collaboration. One person can make a post and others can only comment on it, but not work on changes themselves. As such, our students use Google Docs to properly collaborate. The ‘Full Posting Access’ level does allow editing other posts, but also bypasses the Approval step. Proper collaboration, while maintaining Approval, would go a long way towards making Buffer a platform that where teams can work together. This would include: Multiple individuals can edit the same post Versions are maintained incase a revision needs to be reverted A proper rich text chat based discussion can be had (instead of posting ‘notes’). title: Expose Post Engagement Metrics via the API
body: Buffer Sign in with Buffer Buffer API Roadmap What's New 21 21 Expose Post Engagement Metrics via the API The Buffer GraphQL API currently supports creating and retrieving posts, but the Post type and platform-specific metadata types LinkedInPostMetadata , MastodonPostMetadata , BlueskyPostMetadata ) do not include engagement metrics. We're requesting that engagement/analytics data be added to the API so we can programmatically retrieve post performance without scraping the Buffer UI. What we need Per-post engagement metrics for each platform: - LinkedIn : reactions, comments, impressions, reach, engagement rate - Mastodon : favorites, replies, reblogs - Bluesky : likes, comments, reposts Ideally these would be available as fields on the platform-specific post metadata types, or as a dedicated statistics or engagement field on the Post type. We'd query them on sent posts filtered by status and date range. Current workaround We currently use Playwright browser automation to scrape engagement metrics from the Buffer "Published Posts" dashboard. For each platform, we: 1. Navigate to the channel's published posts page in Buffer 2. Scroll through posts using infinite scroll 3. Parse engagement numbers from the DOM for each post 4. Match scraped data back to our internal history by datetime or text content This approach is brittle (breaks when the UI changes), slow (requires a full browser session per channel), and difficult to maintain. Use case We maintain a history.json file that tracks all posts across LinkedIn, Mastodon, and Bluesky with their engagement metrics. This feeds into an analytics dashboard that correlates social media engagement with website traffic (via PostHog) and SEO performance (via Ahrefs) to optimize our content strategy. Programmatic access to engagement data would make this pipeline reliable and fully automated Comments Join the discussion and help shape the future of Buffer. title: Document LinkedIn character limit and Unicode handling in Buffer docs
body: Buffer Sign in with Buffer Buffer API Roadmap What's New 4 4 Document LinkedIn character limit and Unicode handling in Buffer docs Buffer enforces a 1,248-character limit for LinkedIn posts (based on tests), but this isn't documented anywhere in Buffer's help center or API docs. Users only discover it when a post fails to schedule. This is especially confusing because: LinkedIn's own limit is 3,000 characters Buffer's limit is measured in UTF-16 characters, not visible characters Unicode bold/italic characters (U+1D400–U+1D7FF, commonly used for LinkedIn formatting) count as 2 characters each, so the effective visible character limit is significantly lower than 1,248 Request: Add a documentation page or help article covering: Buffer's character limit per platform (LinkedIn = 1,248 UTF-16 characters) How Unicode formatting characters affect the count (surrogate pairs = 2 units per character) Why a post might fail even when it looks short enough A recommendation or character counter that shows UTF-16 length, not just visible length Right now,w there's no way to predict whether a post will be accepted before hitting "Schedule." A documented limit and an accurate in-app counter would save users significant time and effort. Comments Join the discussion and help shape the future of Buffer. However, because flows may be hijacked (exposing the API key publicly) we need to be sure that the API key cannot be used to directly publish content.
